briefing.headline: 11 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: EMJ, EMJ, SJTREM. Main themes: guideline or formal recommendation, reviews, evidence synthesis. Best early reads: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches; Clinical decision rules for obtaining chest radiography in adult patients presenting to the emergency department with non-traumatic chest pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
briefing.editorialHeadline: From Electrolytes to Airway: Navigating Today's Critical Algorithms
briefing.editorialSummary:
The spectrum of acute care today demands vigilance, whether managing a life-threatening electrolyte derangement or navigating the complexities of undifferentiated illness. When faced with acute hyperkalemia, the focus remains on rapid, evidence-based stabilization to avert cardiac catastrophe. Meanwhile, the management of suspected poisoning requires adopting a 'toxidromic' mindset, using broad frameworks to guide initial workup until the specific agent is identified.

On the procedural front, the literature continues to refine our approach to resuscitation. For instance, recent data on pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest suggest that while physiological metrics like impedance can change with manual pressure augmentation, this intervention has not translated into improved survival outcomes.

Beyond the resuscitation bay, we see valuable shifts in operational care. Consider the utility of expanding senior physician-led assessment units into the community, which can effectively de-escalate unnecessary ED visits, particularly for high-risk patients like those with head injuries. Furthermore, for those managing complex, unstable patients with pulmonary embolism, integrating the latest AHA guidelines into a practical, risk-stratified algorithm remains crucial for decisive action.

dailyEditorial.title: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Timely recognition and appropriate treatment are critical to prevent serious complications such as cardiac arrhythmias and death.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Acute hyperkalaemia is a potentially life-threatening electrolyte disturbance frequently encountered in emergency departments.
dailyEditorial.summary: Acute hyperkalaemia is a potentially life-threatening electrolyte disturbance frequently encountered in emergency departments. Timely recognition and appropriate treatment are critical to prevent serious complications such as cardiac arrhythmias and death. This narrative review summarises current evidence-based and guideline-based recommendations for the emergency management of hyperkalaemia, with a focus on practical challenges and frequently encountered clinical uncertainties.

dailyEditorial.title: Clinical decision rules for obtaining chest radiography in adult patients presenting to the emergency department with non-traumatic chest pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Don't rely on these meta-analyses to dictate immediate changes in practice; the evidence is too heterogeneous and limited to recommend either the Hess or Rothrock criteria definitively.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackled the resource drain caused by routine chest radiography in non-traumatic chest pain patients, noting that imaging is used frequently but yields actionable results in very…
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackled the resource drain caused by routine chest radiography in non-traumatic chest pain patients, noting that imaging is used frequently but yields actionable results in very few cases. The authors pooled data from several studies to compare established clinical decision rules, specifically the Hess and Rothrock criteria. While the meta-analysis provided quantitative comparisons, the findings were somewhat mixed, showing that the Hess rule was more sensitive but might not significantly reduce imaging overall. Crucially, the authors concluded that neither rule is currently recommendable due to limitations in the underlying evidence, including high heterogeneity and the use of retrospective cohorts. This underscores that while the need for better guidelines is clear, the current evidence base is insufficient for definitive adoption.

dailyEditorial.title: Civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury in South-East Norway
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing pTBI, ensure rigorous adherence to established protocols, particularly regarding prophylactic antibiotics, ICP monitoring, and early CTA, even when initial outcomes seem stable.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This report details the management of civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury (pTBI) encountered in South-East Norway.
dailyEditorial.summary: This report details the management of civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury (pTBI) encountered in South-East Norway. While the overall prognosis for survivors appears favorable, the authors noted a concerning gap in adherence to established care guidelines. Specifically, compliance rates for prophylactic antibiotics, antimicrobials, intracranial pressure (ICP) monitoring, and timely surgical revision within the first 12 hours were lower than anticipated. For patients who are deemed potential survivors, the recommendation is to maintain an aggressive management approach, ensuring alignment with standard TBI principles, which includes early vascular imaging via CTA.

dailyEditorial.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When faced with an undifferentiated poisoned patient, utilize this guideline's toxidromic framework to guide initial workup and management, while always cross-referencing with specific poison resources.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service tackles the notoriously difficult scenario of managing patients with suspected but unconfirmed poisoning…
dailyEditorial.summary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service tackles the notoriously difficult scenario of managing patients with suspected but unconfirmed poisoning in the ED. It aims to provide a generalized clinical framework to guide initial assessment, help narrow down potential toxic agents, and direct emergency management efforts. The authors emphasize adopting a toxidromic approach, meaning the management must be highly attuned to the toxicokinetics of potential poisons and how the patient's clinical status might evolve. It's important to note that this guideline is intended as a broad clinical support tool and explicitly does not supersede the detailed, poison-specific guidance found in resources like TOXBASE or the local poisons centre.

dailyEditorial.title: Sequence of advanced airway management and epinephrine administration for paediatric patients with non-shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest
dailyEditorial.takeaway: We therefore aimed to investigate the association between the sequence of AAM and epinephrine administration and outcomes in paediatric patients with OHCA with non-shockable rhythms.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Background Despite the frequent use of both advanced airway management (AAM) and epinephrine during resuscitation, no studies have evaluated the optimal sequencing of these interventions in paediatric out-of-hospital ca…
dailyEditorial.summary: Background Despite the frequent use of both advanced airway management (AAM) and epinephrine during resuscitation, no studies have evaluated the optimal sequencing of these interventions in paediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). We therefore aimed to investigate the association between the sequence of AAM and epinephrine administration and outcomes in paediatric patients with OHCA with non-shockable rhythms. Methods This retrospective observational study analysed data from a multicentre OHCA registry in...

dailyEditorial.title: Community emergency medicine: a service review of patients that sustain head injuries in the community--can early intervention by a senior clinical decision maker provide care closer to home?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Consider the feasibility of expanding senior physician-led community assessment models, especially for high-risk populations like anticoagulated patients with head injuries, to de-escalate unnecessary ED presentations.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review details the operational model of a Physician Response Unit (PRU) that extends senior emergency medicine physician (EMP) decision-making directly into the community, particularly for patients with head injuri…
dailyEditorial.summary: This review details the operational model of a Physician Response Unit (PRU) that extends senior emergency medicine physician (EMP) decision-making directly into the community, particularly for patients with head injuries. The team, comprising physicians and paramedics, operates across a large geographic area, self-tasking or responding to calls to identify patients who can safely be managed at home, thereby avoiding unnecessary Emergency Department attendance. A key component highlighted is the empowerment of EMPs to manage anticoagulated patients in the community, aligning with updated NICE guidelines. The focus is clearly on optimizing care delivery by bringing specialist assessment closer to the patient's residence.

dailyEditorial.title: Prehospital prediction of clinical course in patients with suspected sepsis: a prospective cohort study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: We examined how variables observed by ambulance personnel predicted the clinical course in patients with suspected sepsis.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Background Early prediction of the clinical course may aid clinical decisions for patients with suspected sepsis.
dailyEditorial.summary: Background Early prediction of the clinical course may aid clinical decisions for patients with suspected sepsis. We examined how variables observed by ambulance personnel predicted the clinical course in patients with suspected sepsis. Methods We prospectively collected prehospital data from patients with suspected sepsis in the ambulance service and supplemented the data with in-hospital findings.

dailyEditorial.title: EMCrit 424 – 2026 Crashing PE Update
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use this resource to map the latest AHA guidelines onto the practical, risk-stratified algorithm, ensuring you have a clear pathway for initiating advanced therapies like thrombolysis or thrombectomy.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This update synthesizes the latest evidence for managing hemodynamically significant pulmonary embolism, effectively merging the new AHA consensus guidelines with the established EMCrit risk-stratification framework.
dailyEditorial.summary: This update synthesizes the latest evidence for managing hemodynamically significant pulmonary embolism, effectively merging the new AHA consensus guidelines with the established EMCrit risk-stratification framework. It provides a comprehensive, actionable algorithm covering the spectrum of advanced therapies, including systemic thrombolysis, mechanical thrombectomy, and the role of ECMO. The authors are doing the necessary work to reconcile the sometimes cumbersome alphabetic categorization from the AHA guidelines into practical, bedside-friendly language for the ED setting. Overall, it’s a valuable resource for keeping clinicians current on the rapidly evolving management of massive and submassive PE. It seems designed to streamline decision-making when faced with complex, unstable patients.

dailyEditorial.title: Mothership and Drip-and-Ship Strategies in Mechanical Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The evidence synthesis directly compares the efficacy of direct transfer to a comprehensive center versus staged transfer for AIS-LVO patients.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackles the persistent debate regarding the best out-of-hospital transfer strategy for patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke due to a large vessel occlusion (AIS-LVO).
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackles the persistent debate regarding the best out-of-hospital transfer strategy for patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke due to a large vessel occlusion (AIS-LVO). Specifically, it compares the 'mothership' approach—direct transport to a comprehensive stroke center—against the 'drip-and-ship' model, which involves initial evaluation at a local facility before transfer. The authors synthesized data to evaluate which pathway yields superior clinical outcomes for these critically ill patients. Given the high stakes in time-sensitive stroke care, clarifying the optimal transfer protocol is crucial for improving systemic care coordination.

dailyEditorial.title: Impact of aspirin use on the modified brain injury guidelines for the management of mild traumatic intracranial hemorrhage
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Consider the possibility of safely managing aspirin-using patients with mild tICH at lower acuity levels, but remember this proposal requires further prospective validation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This piece tackles the question of whether pre-existing aspirin use alters the management guidelines for mild traumatic intracranial hemorrhage (tICH).
dailyEditorial.summary: This piece tackles the question of whether pre-existing aspirin use alters the management guidelines for mild traumatic intracranial hemorrhage (tICH). The core finding is a proposal to expand the existing mBIG1 criteria to safely incorporate patients who are on aspirin prior to injury. Essentially, the authors are suggesting that these aspirin-users might be candidates for observation at lower levels of care, potentially avoiding unnecessary transfer to a regional trauma center. While this is a significant clinical suggestion for risk stratification, the authors themselves caution that more prospective data are required to definitively prove this safety expansion.

dailyEditorial.title: A randomised controlled trial of defibrillation with manual pressure augmentation during out-of-hospital cardiac arrest
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not incorporate manual pressure augmentation into standard OHCA protocols based on this trial, as it did not improve survival despite reducing transthoracic impedance.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial assessed the utility of adding manual pressure augmentation (MPA) during defibrillation for patients experiencing initially shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized controlled trial assessed the utility of adding manual pressure augmentation (MPA) during defibrillation for patients experiencing initially shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The study's primary finding, despite the intervention significantly lowering transthoracic impedance, was that MPA did not translate into improved survival or any other measurable clinical outcomes when compared to standard defibrillation protocols. It's important to note that this was a prematurely terminated RCT, which always warrants careful interpretation of the results. While the impedance change is a measurable physiological finding, the lack of survival benefit is the key takeaway for practice.

dailyEditorial.title: Impact of Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Health Care Workers' Physical Health Parameters on Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Performance Quality in Simulated Manikin-Based Settings-A Systematic Review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Consider incorporating objective measures of physical fitness into your local protocols when assessing CPR team readiness, as physical capacity directly influences the ability to sustain high-quality compressions.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes evidence regarding how the physical health and fitness levels of healthcare workers performing CPR actually impact the quality of their resuscitation efforts in simulated environments.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesizes evidence regarding how the physical health and fitness levels of healthcare workers performing CPR actually impact the quality of their resuscitation efforts in simulated environments. The core finding is quite clear: the physical capacity of the provider is a determinant of CPR quality. Specifically, maintaining both adequate depth and sustained frequency of chest compressions demands a combination of underlying strength and endurance in the rescuers. This moves the discussion beyond just protocol adherence to the physical capability of the personnel performing the compressions. It suggests that physical fitness isn't just a general wellness topic but a direct component of resuscitation performance.

dailyEditorial.title: Helicopter Emergency Medical Services attendance is associated with favourable survival outcomes in major trauma: derivation and internal validation of prediction models in a regional trauma system
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The data supports the value of HEMS, especially for severely injured patients with moderate predicted survival, and suggests advanced pre-hospital care components like PHEA may benefit low-probability survivors.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This study looked at a large regional trauma cohort to quantify the survival benefit of receiving Helicopter Emergency Medical Services (HEMS) care for major trauma patients.
dailyEditorial.summary: This study looked at a large regional trauma cohort to quantify the survival benefit of receiving Helicopter Emergency Medical Services (HEMS) care for major trauma patients. Using a W-statistic approach, the authors found that overall, observed 30-day survival exceeded expected survival, suggesting a genuine benefit. This benefit was particularly pronounced in severely injured patients who had a moderate pre-hospital probability of survival. Furthermore, the analysis suggested that advanced pre-hospital interventions, specifically mentioning PHEA, might confer a survival advantage even in patients with a low predicted chance of survival. Overall, the data points toward HEMS providing a measurable, context-specific survival boost in the trauma setting.

dailyEditorial.title: A systematic review of mortality and severe morbidities following newborn chest compressions at birth
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the heterogeneity in existing data, clinicians should remain aware that current outcome estimates for neonatal CC are subject to significant variability, necessitating careful interpretation of any single study's results.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized data on mortality and severe morbidities following neonatal chest compressions (CC) at birth.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesized data on mortality and severe morbidities following neonatal chest compressions (CC) at birth. The authors concluded that the wide variability observed in outcome estimates across different studies cannot be reliably attributed to simple factors like time or geography. They highlighted significant gaps in the current literature, specifically noting the difficulty in cleanly separating deaths due to asphyxia from other causes, and the lack of standardized outcome stratification based on gestational age, birth setting, level of care, or the timing of the compressions themselves. Overall, the review points toward a need for more rigorous, prospective studies to improve the precision and comparability of evidence in neonatal resuscitation protocols.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular resuscitation: an expert practice review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Familiarize yourself with the principles and escalating steps of EVR—from initial vascular access to advanced techniques like aortic balloon occlusion—to better advocate for patients who might benefit from these modalities.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This expert review tackles endovascular resuscitation (EVR), which encompasses several emerging therapies designed to bridge critically ill patients in shock or cardiac arrest toward definitive care.
dailyEditorial.summary: This expert review tackles endovascular resuscitation (EVR), which encompasses several emerging therapies designed to bridge critically ill patients in shock or cardiac arrest toward definitive care. The authors synthesize the current landscape of these techniques, acknowledging the growing adoption despite ongoing debates regarding risk, efficacy, and practical implementation. The review structures its discussion around clinical scenarios, specifically out-of-hospital cardiac arrest and non-compressible hemorrhage. It systematically covers the underlying physiology, technique, efficacy, and safety profile for three escalating interventions: resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta, selective aortic arch perfusion, and extracorporeal cardiopulmonary resuscitation. This provides a comprehensive overview for emergency physicians.

dailyEditorial.title: On the optimal sodium correction rate in hyponatraemia and clinical outcome: a meta-analysis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The meta-analysis leans toward suggesting that a more liberal approach to sodium correction rates might improve survival, but clinicians must remain vigilant regarding the risk of ODS.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This meta-analysis synthesized data from 11 retrospective studies examining the optimal sodium correction rate in emergency department patients with hyponatremia.
dailyEditorial.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized data from 11 retrospective studies examining the optimal sodium correction rate in emergency department patients with hyponatremia. The authors specifically compared outcomes using thresholds of 8, 10, and 12 mmol/L per 24 hours for both mortality and the incidence of osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS). Overall, the findings suggest that rapid sodium correction was associated with improved survival across all tested thresholds. While the data is suggestive, the authors caution that no definitive causal link can be established, and they reiterate that ODS remains a serious, albeit rare, complication to monitor.

dailyEditorial.title: Robotic surgery in colorectal emergencies: a systematic review of current evidence
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering robotic assistance for acute colorectal emergencies, remember that institutional readiness—specifically dedicated training and team structure—is as critical as the equipment itself.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of robotic platforms in the management of colorectal emergencies.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of robotic platforms in the management of colorectal emergencies. The authors conclude that while robotic assistance is indeed feasible and safe for these emergent procedures, its successful implementation is not straightforward. Optimal outcomes appear contingent upon more than just the technology itself; the review strongly emphasizes the necessity for dedicated institutional training programs and the establishment of specialized surgical teams. Essentially, the technology is ready, but the human infrastructure needs to catch up to support its use in acute settings.

dailyEditorial.title: New Guidelines Emphasize Outpatient Treatment of Asymptomatic Hypertension
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Review the specific criteria outlined in the new guidelines to determine if an asymptomatic patient's elevated BP warrants immediate ED medication versus a structured outpatient follow-up plan.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The latest updates from the ACC/AHA and 2025 ACEP guidelines are refining the approach to managing asymptomatic hypertension encountered in the emergency department setting.
dailyEditorial.summary: The latest updates from the ACC/AHA and 2025 ACEP guidelines are refining the approach to managing asymptomatic hypertension encountered in the emergency department setting. Essentially, the guidelines are shifting the emphasis toward optimizing outpatient management rather than defaulting to immediate in-ED pharmacologic intervention for asymptomatic patients. They provide clearer algorithms on when to initiate outpatient medication regimens and what level of follow-up care is necessary after an ED visit. This is a nuanced update that helps triage the appropriate level of care for stable patients presenting with elevated blood pressure without overt symptoms. Understanding these updated thresholds is key to avoiding overtreatment or, conversely, missing appropriate escalation.

dailyEditorial.title: Comparison of three point-of-care ultrasound techniques to confirm endotracheal tube placement: A randomized clinical trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For confirming ETT placement post-RSI, all three ultrasound techniques show high diagnostic performance, but TUS appears to offer the quickest confirmation time.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized clinical trial compared three different point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) techniques for confirming endotracheal tube (ETT) placement following rapid sequence intubation (RSI).
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized clinical trial compared three different point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) techniques for confirming endotracheal tube (ETT) placement following rapid sequence intubation (RSI). The study found that all three assessed ultrasound methods demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy for confirming the ETT's location. Notably, the authors highlighted that transcranial ultrasound (TUS) was the technique associated with the shortest confirmation time among the three modalities tested. This suggests that while multiple methods work well, speed might be a key differentiator in the acute setting.

dailyEditorial.title: Triage administration of sucrose for gastroenteritis in children; a randomized controlled trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not rely on sucrose supplementation as a routine measure to boost oral rehydration intake in pediatric gastroenteritis patients presenting to the ED.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial investigated the utility of administering sucrose solution to children presenting to the emergency department with gastroenteritis.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized controlled trial investigated the utility of administering sucrose solution to children presenting to the emergency department with gastroenteritis. The primary objective was to determine if a single dose of sucrose would enhance oral rehydration intake in this population. The study's conclusion is quite direct: the intervention did not demonstrate an improvement in the amount of oral rehydration fluid consumed by the children in the ED setting. This suggests that adding sucrose proactively in the acute ED setting may not be beneficial for improving fluid intake in pediatric gastroenteritis patients.
